text:
MULTIDISCIPLINARY ONCOLOGY CONSULT NOTE
HEPATOPANCREATOBILIARY TUMOR BOARD / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY

Patient: Margaret Choi
Age: 58 years
Visit Type: New Patient Consultation / Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Presentation
Department: Medical Oncology / Hepatopancreatobiliary Program
Attending: Medical Oncology (Gastrointestinal Oncology)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Choi is a 58-year-old female referred to gastrointestinal medical oncology following new diagnosis of pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma for multidisciplinary evaluation and treatment planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Choi is a 58-year-old female nonsmoker with a background of type 2 diabetes mellitus and no prior oncologic history who presented to her primary care physician approximately 6-8 weeks ago with a constellation of symptoms including progressive mid-epigastric pain radiating to the back, unintentional weight loss of approximately 12 lbs over two months, and new-onset jaundice. She had also noted worsening glycemic control despite an unchanged metformin regimen, which, in retrospect, may have represented a paraneoplastic early manifestation of pancreatic exocrine and endocrine dysfunction. Physical examination by her PCP revealed scleral icterus and epigastric tenderness, prompting urgent gastroenterology referral.

Subsequent workup included a multiphasic CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with pancreatic protocol, which demonstrated a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas with associated biliary and pancreatic ductal dilatation (CBD 14 mm, MPD 6 mm). The CT demonstrated contact of the tumor with the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 180 degrees of its circumference as well as abutment of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein confluence over approximately 90 degrees without frank occlusion. Peripancreatic lymph nodes measured up to 1.1 cm. No hepatic lesions and no peritoneal disease were identified.

Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) with fine needle biopsy was subsequently performed by gastroenterology, yielding a core biopsy of the pancreatic head mass. Histopathology confirmed moderately differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreas with desmoplastic stroma, irregular glandular architecture, nuclear atypia, and mucin production. Immunohistochemistry was consistent with a pancreatic primary: CK7 positive, CK20 negative, MUC1 positive, MUC2 negative, CDX2 negative, with loss of SMAD4 (DPC4) expression by IHC. Ki-67 was approximately 35%.

Serum tumor markers were significantly elevated: CA 19-9 was 1,248 U/mL and CEA was 6.4 ng/mL. Total bilirubin at initial gastroenterology evaluation was 7.2 mg/dL, consistent with malignant biliary obstruction.

Comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation Medicine FoundationOne CDx) performed on the EUS-FNB specimen demonstrated KRAS p.G12V (AF 42%), TP53 p.R248W (AF 38%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, SMAD4 p.R361H (AF 35%), ARID1A p.Q1178* (AF 18%), and STK11 p.L67fs*35 (AF 20%), as well as MYC and GATA6 amplification. No BRCA1/2 mutations, no NTRK fusions, MSI-stable, TMB 4 mut/Mb (low), pMMR. No actionable targets with currently approved pancreatic cancer agents were identified.

The patient was discussed at today's multidisciplinary hepatopancreatobiliary tumor board, which included representation from hepatopancreatobiliary surgery, medical oncology, radiation oncology, interventional gastroenterology, diagnostic radiology, and pathology. The clinical, radiologic, and pathologic data were reviewed in detail. The consensus of the tumor board is summarized below.

Prior to this consultation, the patient underwent ERCP with placement of a covered self-expanding metal biliary stent for relief of malignant obstructive jaundice. Total bilirubin post-stenting (measured approximately 2 weeks following the procedure) has decreased to 1.1 mg/dL, indicating successful biliary decompression. The patient has been medically cleared to proceed with systemic therapy.

Ms. Choi presents today accompanied by her husband. She reports continued mid-epigastric discomfort, now somewhat improved, mild fatigue, and decreased appetite. She denies any new symptoms. She is fully ambulatory and independent with all activities of daily living.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus - diagnosed approximately 4 years ago; previously well-controlled on metformin; recent worsening glycemic control attributed to pancreatic ductal obstruction and tumor-associated exocrine/endocrine dysfunction
2. No prior malignancy
3. No prior abdominal surgery
4. No known cardiovascular disease
5. No known pulmonary disease

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

Ms. Choi is a high school librarian. She is married and lives with her husband. She has a daughter who is closely involved in her care. She is a lifelong nonsmoker. She denies alcohol use beyond occasional social use (less than 1 drink per week currently, none since diagnosis). No illicit drug use. She is of Korean descent. BMI 22.4 at last measurement (has lost approximately 12 lbs since onset of symptoms).

---

FAMILY HISTORY

No known family history of pancreatic cancer. No family history of BRCA-associated malignancies (breast, ovarian, prostate, or melanoma). No family history of Lynch syndrome-associated cancers. No other significant hereditary cancer syndromes identified by history. Parents deceased from cardiovascular causes.

---

ALLERGIES

No known drug allergies. No known contrast allergies (tolerated IV contrast for recent CT imaging without adverse reaction).

---

MEDICATIONS

1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 DM; will be held during chemotherapy per protocol and in the perioperative setting)
2. Ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours as needed (prescribed by gastroenterology post-ERCP, for nausea)
3. Multivitamin daily
4. No aspirin, NSAIDs, anticoagulants, or immunosuppressive agents

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Mid-epigastric pain (improving), back pain (improving), anorexia/decreased appetite, unintentional weight loss (~12 lbs over 2 months), fatigue (mild), prior jaundice (resolved post-stenting), prior dark urine and light stools (resolved), new onset poorly controlled blood glucose.
Negative: No fever or chills, no night sweats, no hematemesis or melena, no hematochezia, no change in bowel habits currently, no nausea at present, no dyspnea, no chest pain, no cough, no lower extremity edema, no thromboembolic symptoms.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Alert, well-appearing female in no acute distress. Thin appearing. Cooperative, appropriately engaged in discussion about her diagnosis and treatment options.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 76 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16, temperature 37.0°C, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, weight 128 lbs.
ECOG Performance Status: 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out work of a light or sedentary nature).
HEENT: Scleral icterus is resolved (previously present; now absent following biliary stenting). Oropharynx clear, mucous membranes moist.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft, mild tenderness to deep palpation in the epigastrium. No guarding. No rebound tenderness. No palpable hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. No palpable abdominal mass. No ascites on clinical exam. Active bowel sounds.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing or cyanosis.
Skin: No jaundice. No spider angiomata. No rash.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. No focal neurologic deficits.
Lymph nodes: No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy in axillary, inguinal, or cervical chains.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Recent labs reviewed (obtained within the past 2 weeks post-stenting):
- Total bilirubin: 1.1 mg/dL (down from 7.2 mg/dL pre-stenting)
- Direct bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- AST: 42 U/L
- ALT: 38 U/L
- Alkaline phosphatase: 148 U/L (decreasing trend)
- Albumin: 3.6 g/dL
- Total protein: 6.8 g/dL
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL
- eGFR: >60 mL/min/1.73 m2
- CBC: WBC 7.4 x10^9/L, hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia, likely anemia of chronic disease), platelets 312 x10^9/L
- CA 19-9: 1,248 U/mL (markedly elevated)
- CEA: 6.4 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
- HbA1c: 8.9% (suboptimal glycemic control)
- INR: 1.1
- Lipase: 28 U/L (normal, within reference range following biliary decompression)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Multiphasic CT Abdomen/Pelvis with Pancreatic Protocol (reviewed at tumor board with diagnostic radiology):
A 3.8 cm hypoenhancing pancreatic head/uncinate process mass is present. The tumor contacts the SMA over approximately 180 degrees of its circumference. The SMV and portal vein confluence are abutted over approximately 90 degrees with loss of the fat plane but without frank encasement or occlusion. The main pancreatic duct and CBD are dilated (6 mm and 14 mm, respectively); the biliary stent is now in place. No discrete hepatic lesions. No peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites. Peripancreatic lymph nodes up to 1.1 cm.

Radiology consensus at tumor board: This tumor configuration meets accepted criteria for borderline resectable disease, specifically by NCCN and AHPBA/Americas Hepato-Pancreato-Biliary Association definitions - SMA contact exceeding 180 degrees and SMV abutment without occlusion. There is no radiographic evidence of distant metastatic disease at this time.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

IMPRESSION:
Ms. Choi is a 58-year-old female with newly diagnosed borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process, clinical stage T3 N1 M0 (Stage III, AJCC 8th edition), by multiphasic CT criteria, with SMA abutment over >180 degrees and SMV abutment without occlusion. Pathologic confirmation by EUS-guided fine needle biopsy shows moderately differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma with loss of SMAD4 expression. Genomic profiling shows a classic PDAC profile (KRAS G12V, TP53, CDKN2A deletion, SMAD4) with no currently actionable targets. CA 19-9 is markedly elevated at 1,248 U/mL. She has undergone ERCP with covered metal biliary stent placement with excellent response - total bilirubin now 1.1 mg/dL. She is ECOG PS 1 and medically cleared to begin systemic therapy.

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD CONSENSUS:

The case was presented before a full multidisciplinary tumor board including HPB surgery, medical oncology, radiation oncology, interventional gastroenterology, radiology, and pathology. All members reviewed the available imaging and pathologic data.

Consensus diagnosis: Borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, pancreatic head/uncinate process.

Consensus recommendation: Neoadjuvant (preoperative) systemic chemotherapy with modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) is recommended as the preferred approach. The rationale for neoadjuvant therapy in this setting includes: (1) the borderline resectable vascular anatomy (SMA contact >180 degrees), which confers a high risk of microscopically positive resection margins if surgery were performed upfront; (2) the opportunity to assess tumor biology and systemic disease control prior to committing to major abdominal surgery; (3) the published evidence from PREOPANC, SWOG S1505, and multiple retrospective series supporting improved margin-negative resection rates and overall survival outcomes with neoadjuvant chemotherapy in borderline resectable disease; and (4) the patient's otherwise good performance status and organ function, making her a strong candidate for intensive chemotherapy.

The HPB surgical team has reviewed the imaging and concurs that upfront surgical resection is not recommended at this time given the vascular anatomy. They anticipate that with successful tumor downsizing, the patient may become a candidate for potentially curative resection.

PLAN:

1. Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy:
   - Modified FOLFIRINOX regimen: Oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1; Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1; Irinotecan 150 mg/m2 IV day 1 (dose-reduced from 180 mg/m2 to mitigate toxicity in the modified regimen); 5-Fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours; every 14 days.
   - Target: 8 cycles over approximately 4 months.
   - Pegfilgrastim (Neulasta) prophylaxis will be administered with each cycle given the myelosuppressive potential of this regimen.
   - Antiemetic prophylaxis: ondansetron, dexamethasone, and aprepitant (NK1 antagonist) for each cycle.
   - Oxaliplatin cold precautions counseled (cold-induced dysesthesia).
   - Patient educated on monitoring for and reporting febrile neutropenia, severe diarrhea, and peripheral neuropathy.

2. Biliary Stent:
   - Covered self-expanding metal biliary stent placed at time of ERCP with successful decompression (total bilirubin now 1.1 mg/dL). The stent is expected to maintain biliary patency throughout the neoadjuvant treatment period. Patient counseled on signs and symptoms of stent dysfunction or cholangitis.

3. Glycemic Management:
   - Metformin will be held during chemotherapy given concern for gastrointestinal toxicity overlap and the potential for lactic acidosis in the setting of reduced oral intake. The patient's primary care physician and/or endocrinology will be engaged to manage glucose during the treatment period. Blood glucose monitoring at home is recommended.

4. Restaging Plan:
   - After 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (approximately 2 months): Repeat multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol for interim restaging and CA 19-9 measurement.
   - After 8 cycles: Full restaging CT; multidisciplinary reassessment for surgical candidacy. If the tumor has demonstrated favorable response and maintained or improved resectability, the team will discuss with the patient the option of proceeding directly to resection versus consolidative chemoradiation prior to surgery (per institutional protocol for borderline resectable cases).

5. Nutritional Support:
   - Referral placed for oncology dietitian. Patient is encouraged to maintain caloric and protein intake. Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) may be considered if signs of exocrine insufficiency emerge.

6. Germline Testing:
   - Germline genetic counseling recommended given the CDKN2A homozygous deletion identified on somatic profiling (raises possibility of germline CDKN2A pathogenic variant, associated with familial atypical multiple mole melanoma/FAMMM syndrome and elevated pancreatic cancer risk). Patient has declined germline testing at this time; this will be revisited at a future visit. Patient was counseled that germline results would not change current treatment approach but could have implications for first-degree relatives.

7. Psychosocial Support:
   - Social work consultation placed. This is a devastating new diagnosis, and Ms. Choi and her family have many questions. She appeared engaged and asking appropriate questions today, accompanied by her husband. The emotional weight of this diagnosis was acknowledged.
   - Additional resources offered: cancer support groups, chaplaincy services, and patient education materials on pancreatic cancer.

8. Follow-up:
   - Return to medical oncology clinic for cycle 1 of mFOLFIRINOX initiation. Pre-chemotherapy labs (CBC with differential, CMP, CA 19-9) to be obtained before each cycle.
   - Patient will receive chemotherapy infusions in the outpatient infusion center.
   - 24-hour oncology triage line provided to the patient and family for any urgent concerns between visits.

Risk-benefit discussion was held at length with the patient and her husband. They understand that the goal of neoadjuvant chemotherapy is to attempt tumor downsizing and improve the likelihood of a curative margin-negative resection, and that there is no guarantee that the tumor will respond or that surgery will ultimately be possible. The risks of FOLFIRINOX including myelosuppression, peripheral neuropathy, gastrointestinal toxicity, and fatigue were discussed explicitly. The patient is agreeable and motivated to proceed.

Total time in clinic (including counseling and coordination): approximately 90 minutes.

Case reviewed and management plan endorsed by the multidisciplinary tumor board.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology
Hepatopancreatobiliary Oncology Program
